subject: math
topic_title: APPLICATION OF LINEAR INEQUALITIES IN REAL LIFE
learning_objectives: Understand Application of Linear Inequalities in Real Life and Introduction to Linear Programming.

sections.heading: Greatest and Least Values
sections.content: Example [cite: 343] Draw a diagram to show the region which satisfies the following inequalities. [cite: 344] 5x + y ≥ - 4, x + y ≤ 4, y ≤ x + 2, y – 2x ≥ - 4 [cite: 345] Find the greatest and the least value of the linear function F = x + 2y within the region. [cite: 346] Solution [cite: 347] For the inequality 5x + y ≥ - 4, first draw the line 5x + y = - 4. [cite: 348] When x = 0, y = 4, when x = -1, y = 1 [cite: 349] Add a third point on your own and then draw line 5x + y = -4. [cite: 350] You may need to extend the axes to do this: [cite: 351] Now use a test point such as x = 0, y = 0 [cite: 352] When x = 0, y = 0, then 0 ≥ - 4 is true, so shade the region below the line 5x + y = -4. [cite: 353] For the inequality x + y ≤ 4, first draw the line x + y = 4. [cite: 354] When x = 0, y = 4 and when y = 0, x = 4. [cite: 355] So draw a line that passes through (0, 4) and (4, 0). [cite: 356] Test point: (0, 0), so 0 ≤ 4 is true. Shade the region above the line. [cite: 357] Similarly, for y ≤ x + 2 and y – 2x ≥ - 4, shade the unwanted regions. [cite: 358] The required region is labeled as R as shown. R is also called the feasible region (i.e. the region that satisfies a set of inequalities). [cite: 359] The greatest (maximum) and the least (minimum) of any linear function such as F = x + 2y occurs at the vertices (corner points) of the region which satisfies the given set of the inequalities. [cite: 360] At A(-1, 1) F = x + 2y [cite: 361] ⇒ F = -1 + 2 = 1 [cite: 362] At B(1, 3) F = x + 2y [cite: 363] ⇒ F = 1 + 6 = 7 [cite: 364] At C(2.67, 1.33) F = x + 2y [cite: 365] ⇒ F = 2.67 + 2.66 = 5.33 [cite: 366] At D(0, -4) F = x + 2y [cite: 367] ⇒ F = 0 – 8 = -8 [cite: 368] ∴ F = x + 2y is least at the point D(0, 4). [cite: 369] F = x + 2y is greatest at the point B(1, 3). [cite: 370] Note: The coordinates at point C can also be found by solving the simultaneous equations x + y = 4 and y – 2x = -4, [cite: 371] Which gives x = 83 and y = 43 . [cite: 372] xz [cite: 373] xz [cite: 374] y [cite: 375] y [cite: 376] C [cite: 377] C [cite: 378] B [cite: 379] B [cite: 380] D [cite: 381] D [cite: 382] A [cite: 383] A [cite: 384] x + y = 4 [cite: 385] x + y = 4 [cite: 386] y = x + 2 [cite: 387] y = x + 2 [cite: 388] R [cite: 389] R [cite: 390] 1 [cite: 391] 2 [cite: 392] 3 [cite: 393] 4 [cite: 394] 5 [cite: 395] 6 [cite: 396] -1 [cite: 397] -2 [cite: 398] -3 [cite: 399] 1 [cite: 400] 2 [cite: 401] 3 [cite: 402] 4 [cite: 403] -1 [cite: 404] -2 [cite: 405] -3 [cite: 406] -4 [cite: 407] 1 [cite: 408] 2 [cite: 409] 3 [cite: 410] 4 [cite: 411] 5 [cite: 412] 6 [cite: 413] -1 [cite: 414] -2 [cite: 415] -3 [cite: 416] 1 [cite: 417] 2 [cite: 418] 3 [cite: 419] 4 [cite: 420] -1 [cite: 421] -2 [cite: 422] -3 [cite: 423] -4 [cite: 424] 5x + y = 4 [cite: 425] 5x + y = 4 [cite: 426] y = 2x = 4 [cite: 427] y = 2x = 4 [cite: 428]

sections.heading: Linear Programming
sections.content: In many real-life situations in business and commerce there are restrictions or constraints , which can affect decision-making. [cite: 430] Typical restrictions might be the amount of money available for a project, storage constraints, or the number of skilled people in a labour force. [cite: 431] In this section we will see that problems involving restrictions can often be solved by using the graphs of linear inequalities. [cite: 432] This method is called linear programming . Linear programming can be used to solve many realistic problems. [cite: 433] Example 1 [cite: 434] A student has N500. She buys pencils at N50 each and erasers at N20 each. [cite: 435] She gets at least five of each and the money spent on pencils is over N100 more than that spent on erasers. [cite: 436] Find a. How many ways the money can be spent, [cite: 437] b. The greatest number of pencils that can be bought, [cite: 438] c. The greatest number of erasers that can be bought. [cite: 439] Let the student buy x pencils at N50 and y erasers at N20. [cite: 440] From the first two sentences, [cite: 441] 50x + 20y ≤ 500 [cite: 442] ⇒ 5x + 2y < 50 (1) [cite: 443] Since she gets at least five of each, [cite: 444] x ≥ 5 (2) [cite: 445] y ≥ 5 (3) [cite: 446] From the third sentence, [cite: 447] ⇒ 5x – 2y > 10 [cite: 448] Inequalities (1), (2), (3) and (4) are shown below [cite: 449] x = 5 [cite: 450] x = 5 [cite: 451] 30 [cite: 452] 30 [cite: 453] Number of erasers [cite: 454] Number of erasers [cite: 455] 20 [cite: 456] 20 [cite: 457] 5x – 2y = 10 [cite: 458] 5x – 2y = 10 [cite: 459] 10 [cite: 460] 10 [cite: 461] y = 5 [cite: 462] y = 5 [cite: 463] ⋮ [cite: 464] ⋮ [cite: 465] ⋮ [cite: 466] ⋮ [cite: 467] 5x + 2y = 50 [cite: 468] 5x + 2y = 50 [cite: 469] 10 [cite: 470] 10 [cite: 471] 5z [cite: 472] 5z [cite: 473] 0z [cite: 474] 0z [cite: 475] Number of pencils [cite: 476] Number of pencils [cite: 477] The solution set of the four inequalities is given by the twelve points marked inside the shaded region. [cite: 478] For example, the point (7, 6) shows that the student can buy seven pencils and six erasers and still satisfy the restrictions on the two variables. [cite: 479] Hence there are twelve ways of spending the money. [cite: 480] The greatest number of pencils that can be bought is eight, corresponding to the point (8, 5) [cite: 481] The greates number of erasers is nine, corresponding to the point (6, 9). [cite: 482] Example 2 [cite: 483] To start a new transport company, a businessman needs at least 5 buses and 10 minibuses. [cite: 484] He is not able to run more than 30 vehicles altogether. [cite: 485] A bus takes up 3 units of parking space, a minibus takes up to 1 unit of parking space and there are only 54 units available. [cite: 486] If x and y are the numbers of buses and minibuses respectively, [cite: 487] Write down four inequalities which represent the restrictions on the businessman [cite: 488] Draw a graph that shows a region representing possible values x and y. [cite: 489] a. from the first sentence, [cite: 490] x ≥ 5 [cite: 491] y ≥ 10 [cite: 492] From the second sentence, [cite: 493] x + y ≤ 30 [cite: 494] from the third sentence, [cite: 495] 3x + y ≤ 54 [cite: 496] b. in the figure below, R is the region that contains the possible values of x and y. [cite: 497] x = 5 [cite: 498] x = 5 [cite: 499] 3x + y = 54 [cite: 500] 3x + y = 54 [cite: 501] 30 [cite: 502] 30 [cite: 503] 20 [cite: 504] 20 [cite: 505] R [cite: 506] R [cite: 507] y = 10 [cite: 508] y = 10 [cite: 509] 10 [cite: 510] 10 [cite: 511] x + y = 30 [cite: 512] x + y = 30 [cite: 513] 30 [cite: 514] 30 [cite: 515] 10z [cite: 516] 10z [cite: 517] 20 [cite: 518] 20 [cite: 519] 0z [cite: 520] 0z [cite: 521]
